unit.subject: English
unit.unitOverview: Welcome to Unit 6! This unit is all about the people in our society — the many workers, leaders, and helpers who make a community work. You will meet engineers and carpenters, governors and senators, artists and photographers, and you will learn about refugees, immigrants, and the roles we all play. Along the way you will use the Present Simple to describe jobs, should and mustn't to give advice and rules, and question words to interview people about their work. By the end, you will be able to describe the helpers in your own town — just like Sarah does in this unit's story, “The Community Parade”.
unit.learningPath:
Start with the Vocabulary file. Learn one group of words each day (there are four groups).
Work through the eight short lessons in the Grammar file, one at a time.
Read the Story, “The Community Parade”, and answer the questions.
Work through the four skills in this lesson: Reading & Listening → Speaking → Writing → Activities.
Finish with the Self-Assessment to see what you have learned.

visual.alt: Learners exploring the roles and contributions of people in society

vocabularyGroups.title: People Who Share Stories and News

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A person who cleans and takes care of a building.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The janitor keeps our school clean and tidy.
practiceSentences: The janitor keeps our school clean and tidy.
The janitor mops the long corridor after the children leave.
Where does the janitor keep the cleaning supplies?
"Our janitor works harder than anyone," said the head teacher.
I thanked the janitor for finding my lost scarf.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: j - a - n - i - t - o - r
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'janitor', use it in a sentence, and connect it to People in Society.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A person who builds things from wood.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The carpenter made a beautiful wooden table.
practiceSentences: The carpenter made a beautiful wooden table.
The carpenter built new shelves for the school library.
What tools does a carpenter carry in his belt?
"Ask the carpenter to repair the bench," said the imam.
I want to be a carpenter like my grandfather.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: c - a - r - p - e - n - t - e - r
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'carpenter', use it in a sentence, and connect it to People in Society.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A person who does hard work, often with their hands.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The laborer helped build the school walls.
practiceSentences: The laborer helped build the school walls.
The laborer loaded heavy sacks of cement onto the truck.
What does a laborer wear to stay safe on site?
"A laborer's hands tell of honest work," said Grandfather.
My neighbour works as a laborer on the new road.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: l - a - b - o - r - e - r
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'laborer', use it in a sentence, and connect it to People in Society.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A person who designs or builds machines, roads, or buildings.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The engineer drew plans for a new bridge.
practiceSentences: The engineer drew plans for a new bridge.
An engineer checked the dam after the heavy rains.
How many years does it take to become an engineer?
"The engineer fixed the school generator," said the caretaker.
I met an engineer who builds wells for dry villages.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: e - n - g - i - n - e - e - r
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'engineer', use it in a sentence, and connect it to People in Society.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A person who buys and sells things, often in a market.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The merchant sold fruit and vegetables at the market.
practiceSentences: The merchant sold fruit and vegetables at the market.
The merchant arranged colourful scarves on his market stall.
What does the merchant sell besides dates and honey?
"A fair merchant never cheats his customers," said Father.
I bought a kite from the friendly merchant at the bazaar.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: m - e - r - c - h - a - n - t
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'merchant', use it in a sentence, and connect it to People in Society.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A person who leads and makes decisions for a city or region.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The governor visited our school and gave a speech.
practiceSentences: The governor visited our school and gave a speech.
The governor opened the new hospital with a short speech.
What decisions does a governor make for the region?
"The governor promised cleaner parks," reported the newspaper.
We wrote letters to the governor about our broken playground.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: g - o - v - e - r - n - o - r
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'governor', use it in a sentence, and connect it to People in Society.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A person chosen to help make the laws for a country.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The senator spoke about new school rules.
practiceSentences: The senator spoke about new school rules.
The senator listened carefully to the farmers' worries.
How does a senator help write new laws?
"Our senator visited the school today," announced the head teacher.
I asked the senator a question about clean water.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: s - e - n - a - t - o - r
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'senator', use it in a sentence, and connect it to People in Society.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A person who helps people understand and follow the law.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The lawyer helped the family solve their problem.
practiceSentences: The lawyer helped the family solve their problem.
The lawyer explained the papers slowly so everyone understood.
When should a family ask a lawyer for help?
"A good lawyer defends people fairly," said my aunt.
My cousin studies to become a lawyer in the city.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: l - a - w - y - e - r
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'lawyer', use it in a sentence, and connect it to People in Society.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: The group of people trained to protect a country.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The military helped the town during the flood.
practiceSentences: The military helped the town during the flood.
The military brought clean water after the earthquake.
What different jobs do people do in the military?
"My brother serves in the military," said Ishmael proudly.
We watched the military band march past on national day.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: m - i - l - i - t - a - r - y
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'military', use it in a sentence, and connect it to People in Society.

dictionaryLinks.groupTitle: People Who Share Stories and News
dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A person who makes art, like drawings or paintings.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The artist painted a big picture of a lion.
practiceSentences: The artist painted a big picture of a lion.
The artist used bright colours to paint the sunset.
Which artist made the mural on the library wall?
"Every artist starts with simple sketches," said Miss Noor.
I felt like a real artist when my picture was displayed.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: a - r - t - i - s - t
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'artist', use it in a sentence, and connect it to People in Society.

dictionaryLinks.groupTitle: People Who Share Stories and News
dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A person who takes pictures with a camera.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The photographer took pictures of the birds.
practiceSentences: The photographer took pictures of the birds.
The photographer waited hours to snap the shy deer.
What kind of camera does the school photographer use?
"Stand still while the photographer counts to three," said Mother.
I helped the photographer carry her bags at the wedding.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: p - h - o - t - o - g - r - a - p - h - e - r
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'photographer', use it in a sentence, and connect it to People in Society.

dictionaryLinks.groupTitle: People Who Share Stories and News
dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A person who carries messages from one person to another.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The messenger brought a letter from my aunt.
practiceSentences: The messenger brought a letter from my aunt.
The messenger rode through the night to deliver the news.
Who sent the messenger to the village chief?
"Give this envelope to the messenger," said the clerk.
I acted as the class messenger and carried notes to teachers.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: m - e - s - s - e - n - g - e - r
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'messenger', use it in a sentence, and connect it to People in Society.

dictionaryLinks.groupTitle: People Who Share Stories and News
dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A short piece of writing in a newspaper or magazine.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: I read an article about the tallest building in the world.
practiceSentences: I read an article about the tallest building in the world.
Samira wrote an article about recycling for the school paper.
Did you read the article about the new library?
"This article explains how bees make honey," said Yusuf.
I cut out an interesting article to show my father.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: a - r - t - i - c - l - e
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'article', use it in a sentence, and connect it to People in Society.

dictionaryLinks.groupTitle: People Who Share Stories and News
dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Someone who is brave or helps others.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The firefighter is a hero for saving the cat.
practiceSentences: The firefighter is a hero for saving the cat.
The doctor became a hero after saving the drowning boy.
Who is the hero in your favourite story?
"My mother is my hero," said Aisha proudly.
I felt like a hero when I found my neighbour's keys.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: h - e - r - o
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'hero', use it in a sentence, and connect it to People in Society.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A person who leaves home because it is not safe.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The refugee found a new home in another country.
practiceSentences: The refugee found a new home in another country.
The refugee family opened a small shop in our town.
How can our school welcome a new refugee student?
"Every refugee deserves safety and kindness," said the imam.
We collected warm blankets for refugee children last winter.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: r - e - f - u - g - e - e
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'refugee', use it in a sentence, and connect it to People in Society.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A person who moves to live in another country.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: My neighbour is an immigrant from a faraway land.
practiceSentences: My neighbour is an immigrant from a faraway land.
The immigrant doctor learned the local language very quickly.
What challenges does an immigrant face in a new country?
"My grandfather arrived as an immigrant with one suitcase," said Father.
Our class heard an immigrant tell her brave journey story.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: i - m - m - i - g - r - a - n - t
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'immigrant', use it in a sentence, and connect it to People in Society.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A person who pays to live in a house or apartment.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The tenant pays rent to the landlord every month.
practiceSentences: The tenant pays rent to the landlord every month.
The new tenant moved into the flat above the bakery.
Does the tenant help water the shared garden too?
"A good tenant keeps the house tidy," said the landlord.
My uncle is a tenant in a building near the mosque.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: t - e - n - a - n - t
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'tenant', use it in a sentence, and connect it to People in Society.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A person who buys or uses things.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: Every consumer wants good quality for their money.
practiceSentences: Every consumer wants good quality for their money.
A smart consumer compares prices before buying anything.
What should a consumer check on a food label?
"Every consumer has the right to complain," said the shopkeeper.
I became a careful consumer after wasting my pocket money once.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: c - o - n - s - u - m - e - r
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'consumer', use it in a sentence, and connect it to People in Society.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A man who is not married.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: My uncle is a bachelor who lives on his own.
practiceSentences: My uncle is a bachelor who lives on his own.
The bachelor next door cooks simple meals for himself.
Is the new teacher a bachelor or a family man?
"Our bachelor uncle visits us every Friday," said Samira.
My eldest cousin is still a bachelor and lives with Grandfather.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: b - a - c - h - e - l - o - r
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'bachelor', use it in a sentence, and connect it to People in Society.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Something that belongs to you or is about you.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: She wrote about her personal story in her diary.
practiceSentences: She wrote about her personal story in her diary.
Aisha keeps her personal diary locked in a drawer.
May I ask you a personal question about your family?
"Keep your personal things inside your bag," said Mrs Ali.
My personal goal is to read twenty books this year.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: p - e - r - s - o - n - a - l
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'personal', use it in a sentence, and connect it to People in Society.

rubrics.level4: Uses target words accurately and naturally

rubrics.level1: Capital letters and punctuation need support

outcomes.learningOutcome: Name and describe at least twenty jobs and roles that people play in society.
Explain what different workers and leaders do, using the Present Simple.
Give advice and state rules using should and mustn't.
Ask and answer interview questions using who, what, where, why, and how.
Use prepositions like across, towards, near, and by to say where people are and where they go.
Tell a short story about the past using the Past Simple and Past Continuous.
outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: Independent writing, presentation or project evidence

outcomes.learningOutcome: Write a role reflection, a short newspaper article, and an advice poster.
outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: Independent writing, presentation or project evidence

outcomes.learningOutcome: Explain the difference between a refugee and an immigrant, and say why we welcome both.
outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: Independent writing, presentation or project evidence

readings.title: The People of Our Town
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript: Every town is full of people who play important roles. Early in the morning, the janitor cleans the school before the pupils arrive. In the market, a merchant sells fresh fruit while a carpenter mends a wooden stall. Near the river, an engineer checks the new bridge, and a laborer carries heavy stones. At the town hall, the governor reads a new plan, and a lawyer helps a family understand the law. We do not always notice these people, but our town could not work without them. Every one of them helps us to live, to learn, and to stay safe.

readings.title: Two Neighbours: A Refugee and an Immigrant
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript: Samira and her family are refugees. They left their home because it was not safe, and they travelled a long way to find peace. Next door lives Noah, an immigrant. He moved to the town to start a new business and build a better life. Samira's mother says, “A refugee runs from danger. An immigrant walks towards a dream. We should welcome both, because everyone deserves a safe home and a kind neighbour.”

readings.title: An Interview with a Community Helper
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript:
Reporter: “Good morning, Mr Musa. What does an engineer do?”
Mr Musa: “Good morning! An engineer designs and builds things, like roads, bridges, and machines. I am building a new bridge for our town.”
Reporter: “Where do you work?”
Mr Musa: “I work near the river, and sometimes in an office. Today I am standing by the water, checking the plans.”
Reporter: “Why is your work important?”
Mr Musa: “Because a strong bridge helps people cross safely. You mustn't build in a hurry — you should plan carefully and check everything twice.”

readings.title: The Community Parade
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript:
It was the morning of the town's Unity Parade in Northern Nigeria. Sarah stood beside her mother, watching people gather across the dusty street. Everyone was getting ready for the big day. Sarah's school had been chosen to make posters about the important jobs in their community.
Sarah held up a sign. “This one says, ‘Engineer — builds bridges and machines.’”
Her friend Ishmael smiled. “Mine says, ‘Janitor — keeps our school clean.’”
Sarah looked at her brother. “Ishmael, where is your sign?”
He laughed. “I made two! One for the military, because they protect us, and one for the refugees who moved here last year.”
“That is kind,” their teacher said. “We must show respect to all people — especially those who help us, and those who need our help.”
The class walked towards the centre of town. They passed the bakery, the market, and the mosque, where families waved and took photos. Everyone stood by the road, ready to watch.
The parade began with the mayor, followed by the local governor. People clapped. “He works so hard,” said an old man.
Next came the military, marching proudly. A small boy asked, “Who are they?”
“They protect our country,” his mother said. “You mustn't forget their service.”
Then a woman held up a photo. “This is my son. He is a lawyer now!”
Sarah whispered to Ishmael, “What does a lawyer do?”
“He helps people solve their problems,” Ishmael answered.
Suddenly, loud music played. A group of dancers went past. Among them was an artist, painting live on a big canvas.
“Wow!” said Ishmael. “That is like magic.”
“It is hard work,” their teacher said. “The artist must plan carefully and work with great care.”
“Look!” Sarah pointed. “Here comes the photographer. She is taking pictures of everything.”
The woman smiled. “Say cheese!” They posed and giggled.
Then a group of immigrants came marching. They carried flags and smiled. A young man held a sign: “From Sudan to Nigeria — I found a home.”
Sarah clapped. “Mama, what is the difference between an immigrant and a refugee?”
Her mother explained, “A refugee leaves home to stay safe. An immigrant moves to build a new life. We should welcome both.”
In the next part of the parade, someone shouted, “Here come the helpers!”
A carpenter walked with his tools. A merchant pushed a cart full of fruit. A laborer waved, his shirt dusty from the morning's work. And a janitor walked proudly in his clean uniform.
Sarah's sign said, “Every helper is a hero.”
A boy behind her asked, “But isn't a hero someone who saves people?”
“Yes,” said Sarah. “But heroes are not only in stories. Some clean our schools, fix our roads, or bring us news.”
Just then, a bicycle bell rang. It was a messenger, holding a stack of newspapers.
“Read today's top article!” he shouted. “Local senator visits school and praises the students!”
Their teacher took a paper and read the first line aloud: “Young citizens learn about jobs and respect.”
Back at school, the class talked about everything they had seen.
“What did you learn?” the teacher asked.
“I learned what a tenant is,” said Ishmael. “A person who rents a home.”
“And a consumer,” said Sarah. “That is someone who buys things — like me at the shop!”
The teacher smiled. “Good. And remember: everyone has a role. You should thank the people around you. You mustn't ignore them.”
Later that day, Sarah wrote in her journal. She kept it in a personal place where no one else could read it:
“Today I saw artists, janitors, and even the military. Some wore fine clothes. Others wore work shoes. But all of them help us to live, to grow, and to stay safe. We walk past them every day, but today, we noticed them.”
She paused, then added one more line:
“Next time I see the janitor, I will say thank you. Because heroes don't always wear capes. Sometimes, they carry brooms.”

grammar.title: Present Simple
grammar.explanation: We use the Present Simple to talk about routines, facts, and things that happen again and again. It is perfect for describing jobs, because most jobs are the same every day: “The janitor cleans the school every morning.”
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
I / you / we / they + base verb:  “They build roads.”
he / she / it + verb + s / es:  “The engineer designs bridges.”
Negatives use don't / doesn't:  “She doesn't work on Fridays.”
Questions use Do / Does:  “Does a lawyer help people?”
Add es for verbs ending in -sh, -ch, -x, or -o: “watches”, “goes”, “fixes”. If a verb ends in a consonant + y, change y to i and add es: “carry” → “carries”.
grammar.commonMistake:
Mistake: “She play soccer.”  ✗
Fix: For he / she / it, don't forget the s: “She plays soccer.”  ✓
Mistake: “Does she plays soccer?”  ✗   Fix: after does, use the base verb: “Does she play soccer?”  ✓
grammar.memoryTip: “He, she, it — add an s to it!” Think of the s as a little superhero cape that only he, she, and it get to wear.
grammar.practice: Write the correct Present Simple form of the verb in brackets. | The cat (sleep) ______ on the sofa every afternoon. | My mother (cook) ______ dinner in the evening. | They (play) ______ football every weekend. | The teacher (give) ______ homework on Fridays. | She (not like) ______ broccoli.

grammar.title: Imperatives
grammar.explanation: Imperatives are sentences that give a command, an instruction, or a piece of advice. They tell someone what to do — or what not to do. We use them for rules, recipes, directions, and kind advice, like “Be kind to new immigrants.”
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Start with the verb. You do not need the word “you”:  “Listen carefully.”
For a negative command, put don't before the verb:  “Don't forget your lunch.”
Use please to be polite:  “Please close the door.”
Use let's to suggest doing something together:  “Let's help the new pupil.”
grammar.commonMistake:
Mistake: “You listen to the teacher.”  ✗
Fix: An imperative does not need a subject. Just start with the verb: “Listen to the teacher.”  ✓
grammar.memoryTip: Think of a coach shouting to the team: “Run! Pass! Shoot!” A coach never says “You run” — just the action word. Imperatives are friendly, quick reminders.
grammar.practice: Complete each command with the correct imperative verb from the brackets. | ______ your hands before eating. (wash) | ______ your voice when the teacher is talking. (lower) | ______ to your parents. (listen) | ______ your toys after playing. (put away) | ______ to be on time. (try)

grammar.title: Modal Verbs — should and mustn't
grammar.explanation: Modal verbs help us talk about advice and rules. Should gives friendly advice — a good idea. Mustn't is a strong rule — something you are not allowed to do. Both help us live well together in a community.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
should + base verb (advice):  “You should thank the helpers.”
mustn't + base verb (a rule, not allowed):  “You mustn't ignore a tenant's needs.”
should can ask a question:  “Should I help you?”
The opposite of should is shouldn't:  “You shouldn't be rude.”
grammar.commonMistake:
Mistake: “You shouldn't to go.”  ✗
Fix: After a modal verb, use the base verb only — no “to”: “You shouldn't go.”  ✓   Also remember: must = you have to; mustn't = you must not.
grammar.memoryTip: Should is a friendly guide saying, “Hey, this is a good idea!” Mustn't is a strict red sign saying, “Stop! You can't do this!”
grammar.practice: Complete each sentence with should or mustn't. | You ______ forget to brush your teeth before bed. | We ______ help our friends when they need it. | You ______ play video games all night. | Students ______ listen to the teacher in class.

grammar.title: Prepositions of Direction and Place
grammar.explanation: Prepositions are small words that tell us where something is or which way it is going. They make our sentences clear: “The refugee walked towards the building” tells us the direction, while “The book is on the shelf” tells us the place.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Direction (where something is going): towards, across, by.
Place (where something is): on, in, near.
Direction example:  “She walked across the street.”
Place example:  “The school is near the library.”  /  “The toys are in the box.”
grammar.commonMistake:
Mistake: “The apples are on the basket.”  ✗
Fix: The apples are inside, so use in: “The apples are in the basket.”  ✓
grammar.memoryTip: Draw a little map in your mind: in = inside a box, on = resting on a table, near = close beside, towards = walking to a sign, across = over a bridge, by = standing side by side.
grammar.practice: Use a word from the box to complete each sentence:  across  ·  towards  ·  on  ·  in  ·  near  ·  by | The bird flew ______ the tree. | The dog is sitting ______ the grass. | She walked ______ the street to get to the store. | The pencil is ______ the table. | The library is ______ my school. | He ran ______ the finish line.

grammar.title: Past Simple
grammar.explanation: The Past Simple tells us about actions that already finished. We use it for stories and for the lives of real people: “The lawyer helped many families.” It lets us share what happened yesterday, last week, or long ago.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Regular verbs: add -ed:  “play” → “played”, “start” → “started”.
Verbs ending in -e: just add -d:  “like” → “liked”.
Irregular verbs change completely:  “go” → “went”, “see” → “saw”, “have” → “had”.
The verb stays the same for every subject:  “I played”, “she played”, “they played”.
grammar.commonMistake:
Mistake: “He help many people.”  ✗
Fix: The action is finished, so change the verb: “He helped many people.”  ✓   And don't forget the subject: not “Gave a speech” but “She gave a speech.”
grammar.memoryTip: The Past Simple is a time machine. When you use it, you travel back to something that already happened. Remember: Regular = -ed, Irregular = a special form you learn by heart.
grammar.practice: Write the Past Simple form of the verb in brackets. | Last summer, I ______ (visit) my grandparents. | The teacher ______ (give) us a test last week. | They ______ (play) video games all evening. | She ______ (make) a delicious cake for the party. | He ______ (write) a letter to his friend.

grammar.title: Past Continuous + Descriptive Verbs and Similes + Question Words
grammar.explanation: The Past Continuous describes an action that was happening at a certain moment in the past. It is great for stories, especially when one action is interrupted by another: “The merchant was working when the rain started.”
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
was / were + verb + -ing.
Use was with I, he, she, it:  “I was reading.”
Use were with you, we, they:  “They were playing.”
Often used with when or while:  “She was studying when her friend called.”
grammar.commonMistake:
Mistake: “They were play in the yard.”  ✗
Fix: Don't forget the -ing: “They were playing in the yard.”  ✓   Also, state verbs like *know* and *love* do not use -ing: not “I was knowing” but “I knew”.
grammar.memoryTip: Think of the Past Continuous as a movie of the past. Say “I was watching…” and picture the action still unfolding, until something interrupts it.
grammar.practice: Write the Past Continuous form of the verb(s) in brackets. | The cat ______ (sleep) on the couch when the doorbell rang. | We ______ (play) outside when it started to rain. | She ______ (read) a book while he ______ (watch) TV. | Lesson 7:  Descriptive Verbs and Similes | What it means and why it matters | A descriptive verb paints a clearer picture of an action, and a simile compares two things using like or as. Together they make your writing exciting: “The janitor worked like a machine” shows us he was fast and never stopped. | How to build it | Choose a strong verb instead of a plain one:  “run” → “dash” or “sprint”. | Think of something similar to compare it to. | Join them with like or as:  “He dashed like a cheetah.” | Put it in a full sentence:  “The dog barked like a lion.” | A common mistake | Mistake: “He is like a hero.”  ✗ (this describes who he is, not what he does) | Fix: Use like to compare an action: “He fights like a hero.”  ✓   Also, swap weak verbs for strong ones: not “ran fast” but “sprinted like the wind.” | Memory tip | A simile is a way to paint a picture with words. Ask yourself: “What is this action like?” Then use like or as to make the picture clear. | Practice 7 | Finish each sentence with your own descriptive verb and simile. (Example answers are in the key.) | The cat jumped ______ a ______ . | She wrote her story ______ a ______ . | The thunder rumbled ______ a ______ . | Lesson 8:  Question Words | What it means and why it matters | Question words help us ask for information. Good learners ask lots of questions! We use them in interviews and conversations: “What does a lawyer do?”, “Where does an engineer work?” | How to build it | Who = people  ·  What = things or ideas  ·  Where = places | When = time  ·  Why = reasons  ·  How = the way something is done | Structure: Question word + helping verb + subject + main verb:  “What do you like to eat?” | Who can also be the subject:  “Who is coming?” | A common mistake | Mistake: “What you like?”  ✗ | Fix: Don't forget the helping verb do / does: “What do you like?”  ✓ | Memory tip | Question words are little detectives. Each one hunts for a different clue: Who (people), What (things), Where (places), When (time), Why (reasons), How (methods). | Practice 8 | Fill each blank with the correct question word. | ______ is your best friend? | ______ do you go to the park? | ______ do you want for your birthday? | ______ does your family live? | ______ do you like ice cream?

speaking.title: Speaking 1: Describe a Job
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
Choose one job from your Vocabulary list. Get ready to say three sentences about it, using the Present Simple:
“A ______ is a person who ______.”
“A ______ works ______ (where).”
“A ______ helps our community by ______.”
Record yourself. Check: Did you add the s for “he/she” (“A lawyer helps”)? Did you speak clearly?
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Help me practise describe a job and give feedback on clarity, vocabulary and complete sentences.

speaking.title: Speaking 2: The Interview Game
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
Play with your AI tutor or a partner. One person chooses a job in secret. The other asks questions to guess it:
“What do you do?”  →  “I build things from wood.”
“Where do you work?”  →  “I work in a workshop.”
“What do you use?”  →  “I use a hammer and saw.”
Guess the job! (A carpenter.) Then swap turns.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Help me practise the interview game and give feedback on clarity, vocabulary and complete sentences.

speaking.title: Speaking 3: Giving Advice
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
Practise giving kind advice about how to treat people in society, using should and mustn't:
“You should thank the people who help you.”
“You should welcome a new immigrant to your school.”
“You mustn't ignore someone who needs help.”
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Help me practise giving advice and give feedback on clarity, vocabulary and complete sentences.

speaking.title: Speaking 4: Vocabulary Expert Interview
speaking.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.2 curriculum expansion
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines: Choose six unit words. Ask and answer a clear question about each word using complete sentences.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Help me practise vocabulary expert interview and give feedback on clarity, vocabulary and complete sentences.

speaking.title: Speaking 5: Explain and Respond
speaking.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.2 curriculum expansion
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines: Explain one important unit idea, give a reason with because, and respond to two follow-up questions.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Help me practise explain and respond and give feedback on clarity, vocabulary and complete sentences.

speaking.title: Speaking 6: Three-Minute Unit Talk
speaking.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.2 curriculum expansion
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines: Plan and record a three-minute talk using an opening, three connected ideas with evidence or examples, and a purposeful closing.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Help me practise three-minute unit talk and give feedback on clarity, vocabulary and complete sentences.

writing.title: Writing 1: A Role Reflection Paragraph
writing.promptAndInstructions: Choose one job (for example, a janitor, a lawyer, or an engineer). Write four or five sentences about it in the Present Simple. Say who the person is, what they do, where they work, and why their job matters.
writing.modelText: A strong Grade 4 response uses organised paragraphs, precise unit words, relevant evidence or examples, and a purposeful conclusion about a role reflection paragraph.
writing.successCriteria: I answered the prompt; organised ideas into paragraphs; used precise Unit vocabulary; supported ideas with details or evidence; checked cohesion and punctuation; revised my work
writing.support: Plan a topic sentence and supporting details, then use the word bank and a source model.
writing.extension: Vary sentence openings, compare two ideas, and strengthen the conclusion.

writing.title: Writing 2: A Short Newspaper Article
writing.promptAndInstructions: Write a short article (about six sentences) about a local hero or community helper — for example, a refugee who started a business, or a carpenter who helped rebuild a school. Give your article a headline in capital letters, like the messenger in the story: “LOCAL HERO HELPS THE TOWN.”
writing.modelText: A strong Grade 4 response uses organised paragraphs, precise unit words, relevant evidence or examples, and a purposeful conclusion about a short newspaper article.
writing.successCriteria: I answered the prompt; organised ideas into paragraphs; used precise Unit vocabulary; supported ideas with details or evidence; checked cohesion and punctuation; revised my work
writing.support: Plan a topic sentence and supporting details, then use the word bank and a source model.
writing.extension: Vary sentence openings, compare two ideas, and strengthen the conclusion.

writing.title: Writing 3: An Advice Poster
writing.promptAndInstructions: Make a welcome or safety poster for new immigrants or tenants. Write three pieces of advice using should, and one rule using mustn't. For example: “You should say hello to your neighbours.” / “You mustn't be afraid to ask for help.” Then draw a picture to go with it.
writing.modelText: A strong Grade 4 response uses organised paragraphs, precise unit words, relevant evidence or examples, and a purposeful conclusion about an advice poster.
writing.successCriteria: I answered the prompt; organised ideas into paragraphs; used precise Unit vocabulary; supported ideas with details or evidence; checked cohesion and punctuation; revised my work
writing.support: Plan a topic sentence and supporting details, then use the word bank and a source model.
writing.extension: Vary sentence openings, compare two ideas, and strengthen the conclusion.

writing.title: Writing 4: Connected Paragraph
writing.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.2 curriculum expansion
writing.promptAndInstructions: Write a six-sentence paragraph about the unit. Begin with a topic sentence and join ideas with and, but or because.
writing.modelText: A strong Grade 4 response uses organised paragraphs, precise unit words, relevant evidence or examples, and a purposeful conclusion about connected paragraph.
writing.successCriteria: I answered the prompt; organised ideas into paragraphs; used precise Unit vocabulary; supported ideas with details or evidence; checked cohesion and punctuation; revised my work
writing.support: Plan a topic sentence and supporting details, then use the word bank and a source model.
writing.extension: Vary sentence openings, compare two ideas, and strengthen the conclusion.

writing.title: Writing 5: Information Text
writing.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.2 curriculum expansion
writing.promptAndInstructions: Write a heading and five accurate facts. Use precise unit vocabulary and check spelling and punctuation.
writing.modelText: A strong Grade 4 response uses organised paragraphs, precise unit words, relevant evidence or examples, and a purposeful conclusion about information text.
writing.successCriteria: I answered the prompt; organised ideas into paragraphs; used precise Unit vocabulary; supported ideas with details or evidence; checked cohesion and punctuation; revised my work
writing.support: Plan a topic sentence and supporting details, then use the word bank and a source model.
writing.extension: Vary sentence openings, compare two ideas, and strengthen the conclusion.

writing.title: Writing 6: Reflect and Improve
writing.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.2 curriculum expansion
writing.promptAndInstructions: Choose earlier writing, revise three sentences, add two useful details, and explain one improvement you made.
writing.modelText: A strong Grade 4 response uses organised paragraphs, precise unit words, relevant evidence or examples, and a purposeful conclusion about reflect and improve.
writing.successCriteria: I answered the prompt; organised ideas into paragraphs; used precise Unit vocabulary; supported ideas with details or evidence; checked cohesion and punctuation; revised my work
writing.support: Plan a topic sentence and supporting details, then use the word bank and a source model.
writing.extension: Vary sentence openings, compare two ideas, and strengthen the conclusion.

activities.title: Activities 1: Who Am I? (Match the job)
activities.instructionsAndItems:
Read each clue and write the job.
I clean and take care of buildings. Who am I?
I take pictures with a camera. Who am I?
I help people understand the law. Who am I?
I build things from wood. Who am I?
I carry messages from one person to another. Who am I?
Answers: 1. a janitor  2. a photographer  3. a lawyer  4. a carpenter  5. a messenger
activities.answerSummary: Accept accurate responses supported by unit vocabulary, text evidence or a clear demonstration.

activities.title: Activities 2: Present Simple: Add the ‘s’
activities.instructionsAndItems:
Write each sentence with the correct Present Simple verb.
The engineer ______ (design) bridges.
The children ______ (thank) the helpers.
A merchant ______ (sell) fruit at the market.
The soldiers ______ (protect) the country.
Answers: 1. designs  2. thank  3. sells  4. protect
activities.answerSummary: Accept accurate responses supported by unit vocabulary, text evidence or a clear demonstration.

activities.title: Activities 3: should or mustn't?
activities.instructionsAndItems:
Complete each piece of advice.
You ______ welcome a new immigrant to your class.
You ______ be unkind to people who help you.
We ______ thank the workers who keep our town clean.
You ______ forget the service of the military.
Answers: 1. should  2. mustn't  3. should  4. mustn't
activities.answerSummary: Accept accurate responses supported by unit vocabulary, text evidence or a clear demonstration.

activities.title: Activities 4: Refugee or Immigrant?
activities.instructionsAndItems:
Read each sentence and write refugee or immigrant.
This person left home because there was danger and they needed to be safe.
This person moved to a new country to start a new job and a new life.
Answers: 1. refugee  2. immigrant
activities.answerSummary: Accept accurate responses supported by unit vocabulary, text evidence or a clear demonstration.

activities.title: Activities 5: Vocabulary Sort and Explain
activities.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.2 curriculum expansion
activities.instructionsAndItems: Sort twelve unit words into useful categories. Explain the rule for each category in a complete sentence.
activities.answerSummary: Accept accurate responses supported by unit vocabulary, text evidence or a clear demonstration.

activities.title: Activities 6: Reading Evidence Hunt
activities.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.2 curriculum expansion
activities.instructionsAndItems: Find six important details in the unit texts. Record the detail and explain what it helps the reader understand.
activities.answerSummary: Accept accurate responses supported by unit vocabulary, text evidence or a clear demonstration.

assignments.title: People in Society learning portfolio
assignments.instructions: Submit Writing 1: A Role Reflection Paragraph and a recording of Speaking 1: Describe a Job. Improve both pieces using the Grade 4 rubrics.
assignments.sourceFile: Derived from unit writing and speaking tasks

quizzes.question: What does 'janitor' mean?
quizzes.options: A person who buys and sells things, often in a market. | A person who cleans and takes care of a building. | A person who designs or builds machines, roads, or buildings. | A person who does hard work, often with their hands.
quizzes.correctAnswer: A person who cleans and takes care of a building.
quizzes.explanation: The janitor keeps our school clean and tidy.

quizzes.question: What does 'carpenter' mean?
quizzes.options: A person who builds things from wood. | A person who buys and sells things, often in a market. | A person who designs or builds machines, roads, or buildings. | A person who leads and makes decisions for a city or region.
quizzes.correctAnswer: A person who builds things from wood.
quizzes.explanation: The carpenter made a beautiful wooden table.

quizzes.question: What does 'laborer' mean?
quizzes.options: A person chosen to help make the laws for a country. | A person who buys and sells things, often in a market. | A person who does hard work, often with their hands. | A person who leads and makes decisions for a city or region.
quizzes.correctAnswer: A person who does hard work, often with their hands.
quizzes.explanation: The laborer helped build the school walls.

quizzes.question: What does 'engineer' mean?
quizzes.options: A person chosen to help make the laws for a country. | A person who designs or builds machines, roads, or buildings. | A person who helps people understand and follow the law. | A person who leads and makes decisions for a city or region.
quizzes.correctAnswer: A person who designs or builds machines, roads, or buildings.
quizzes.explanation: The engineer drew plans for a new bridge.

quizzes.question: Choose the missing word from 'The People of Our Town': Early in the morning, the _____ cleans the school before the pupils arrive.
quizzes.explanation: The text says: Early in the morning, the janitor cleans the school before the pupils arrive.
quizzes.origin: Generated from this unit's reading passages

quizzes.question: Choose the missing word from 'The People of Our Town': In the market, a merchant sells fresh fruit while a _____ mends a wooden stall.
quizzes.explanation: The text says: In the market, a merchant sells fresh fruit while a carpenter mends a wooden stall.
quizzes.origin: Generated from this unit's reading passages

quizzes.question: Choose the missing word from 'The People of Our Town': Near the river, an engineer checks the new bridge, and a _____ carries heavy stones.
quizzes.explanation: The text says: Near the river, an engineer checks the new bridge, and a laborer carries heavy stones.
quizzes.origin: Generated from this unit's reading passages

quizzes.question: Choose the missing word from 'An Interview with a Community Helper': What does an _____ do?” Mr Musa: “Good morning!
quizzes.explanation: The text says: What does an engineer do?” Mr Musa: “Good morning!
quizzes.origin: Generated from this unit's reading passages

quizzes.question: Choose the missing word from 'The Community Parade': A _____ pushed a cart full of fruit.
quizzes.explanation: The text says: A merchant pushed a cart full of fruit.
quizzes.origin: Generated from this unit's reading passages

quizzes.question: Choose the missing word from 'The People of Our Town': At the town hall, the _____ reads a new plan, and a lawyer helps a family understand the law.
quizzes.explanation: The text says: At the town hall, the governor reads a new plan, and a lawyer helps a family understand the law.
quizzes.origin: Generated from this unit's reading passages

teacherNotes.note: Model new language aloud, check meaning before independent work, use mixed-response modes, and give specific feedback on complete sentences, text evidence and vocabulary precision.

💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
“Can you read the interview with me and take one of the parts?”
“Can you be a community helper and let me interview you?”
“Can you check my answers to the reading questions?”
💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
“Can you play the Interview Game with me?”
“Can you listen to my sentences about a job and check my grammar?”
“Can you help me think of good advice for welcoming a new pupil?”
💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
“Can you check my role reflection paragraph for Present Simple mistakes?”
“Can you help me write a good headline for my newspaper article?”
“Can you check that my advice poster uses should and mustn't correctly?”
💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
“Can you quiz me on what each worker does?”
“What is the difference between a laborer and an engineer?”
“Can you help me use each word in a Present Simple sentence?”
💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
“What is the difference between a governor and a senator?”
“Can you play an interview game where I ask what a lawyer does?”
“Can you check my sentence about the military?”
💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
“Can you help me describe what an artist does?”
“Can you read a short news article with me and explain the hard words?”
“Who can be a hero in real life, not just in stories?”
💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
“What is the difference between a refugee and an immigrant?”
“Can you check my sentence using the word tenant?”
“How can I make a new immigrant feel welcome?”
💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
“Can you check my grammar answers and explain the ones I got wrong?”
“Why do we say ‘he cleans’ but ‘they clean’?”
“Can you give me five more sentences to practise should and mustn't?”
“Can you turn this present-simple sentence into the past simple with me?”

answerKey.answerOrGuidance: Accept accurate responses supported by unit vocabulary, text evidence or a clear demonstration.

answerKey.answerOrGuidance: A person who cleans and takes care of a building. The janitor keeps our school clean and tidy.
A person who builds things from wood. The carpenter made a beautiful wooden table.
A person who does hard work, often with their hands. The laborer helped build the school walls.
A person who designs or builds machines, roads, or buildings. The engineer drew plans for a new bridge.
A person who buys and sells things, often in a market. The merchant sold fruit and vegetables at the market.
A person who leads and makes decisions for a city or region. The governor visited our school and gave a speech.
A person chosen to help make the laws for a country. The senator spoke about new school rules.
A person who helps people understand and follow the law. The lawyer helped the family solve their problem.
The group of people trained to protect a country. The military helped the town during the flood.
A person who makes art, like drawings or paintings. The artist painted a big picture of a lion.

selfAssessment.statement: I can use prepositions like across, towards, near and by.
